item.title: Evaluating AI Agents Deeplearning.ai Course
item.description: The need to engineer specialised Agents to be effective (best results) and efficient (least waste) is growing. AI inference costs will increase as they accelerate towards energy limits. Worth learning how to evaluate and improve systems built around LLMs. I’m starting from the beginning.

item.title: Recreating A Monkey Island Scene In GenAIpha Language
item.description:
Whenever opportunity arises, I like to introduce highlights from retro computer history to my kids.

My daughter and I are big fans of the Monkey Island games from the nineties. So together, we’re learning how to make an adventure game. We’re learning by recreating one scene from the 1991 classic, Monkey Island 2.

item.title: Introducing Outline Eclipsed Vscode Extension
item.description:
Do you write in markdown? Do you use vscode? (but deep down you miss Eclipse)

What if you wanna re-order sections of your documents? Do you cut-paste sections? Or Alt + Up/Down? Or Something else?

item.title: Misinformation on Dockerhub Rate Limits
item.description:
In April 2025, Docker Inc withdrew plans to further constrain Docker Hub Rate Limits.

So rate limits remain unchanged at:

Account Type Limit
anonymous users 100 pulls per 6 hours per IP address
authenticated users 200 pulls per 6 hour period
paid subscribers Unlimited pulls (up from 5000 pulls per day)

But misinformation remains in AI responses, search results and registry vendor blogs.

item.title: Piri Piri Prawns
item.description:
Ingredients

  1. 400g prawns (200g per person)
  2. 2-3 lemons (zest and juice)
  3. Piri piri chilli oil
  4. Olive oil
  5. Smoked paprika
  6. Peanut butter
  7. Butter
  8. White wine
  9. Creme fraise
  10. English parsley

Steps

  1. Clean the prawns and dry them. Marinade with lemon zest, piri piri chilli oil and a generous seasoning of smoked paprika. Set aside.

item.title: Playing CTF Challenges Coop With Copilot (Part 3)
item.description:
Let’s see if we can apply what we learned in Part 1 and Part 2 to the next level…

Krypton6: Crack A Stream Cipher With A Weak Random Number Generator

Let’s play krypton6.

item.title: Playing CTF Challenges Co-op With Copilot (Part 2)
item.description:
Let’s see if we can apply what we learned in the previous article to greater effect…

Krypton5: Crack The Vigenère Cipher From 3 Intercepted Ciphertexts

Let’s play krypton5. We’ll give Copilot the context of the challenge, excluding the solution from krypton4.Let us see where it leads…

item.title: Playing CTF Challenges Co-op With Copilot
item.description: Github Copilot is at the “Peak of Inflated Expectations” for many, including me. With all the Copilot hype, it’s easy to imagine unicorns assisting programmers in their world of joy!

item.title: How To Pull And Verify Any Public Helm Chart From An OCI Registry
item.description:
When you wanna pull a public helm chart from an OCI registry…

Some public charts aren’t yet published as OCI artifacts. For those charts, one elegant solution is to use the open source Helm Chart OCI Proxy.

item.title: Standardising Private Registry Configuration
item.description:
IT Ops has evolved since the time-honoured practice of “turn it off and on again!”

Aisha’s a Kubernetes Platform Engineer. Every day, she upgrades and rolls back helm charts.

item.title: Exploring Oci Registries By Use Case
item.description: Imagine an organisation has 6 application teams. Each team has their own Kubernetes cluster so they can operate independently on their own cadence. Each cluster has access to pull images from the public internet.

item.title: Exploring OCI Registries: Chapter 2: Install a Public Helm Chart on a Private Kubernetes Cluster
item.description:
When you wanna install a public helm chart on a private kubernetes cluster…

See also How To Pull And Verify Any Public Helm Chart From An OCI Registry

item.title: Exploring OCI Container Registries: Chapter 1: Pull a Public Image from Kubernetes
item.description:
Let’s begin with one of the most common use cases of a Container Registry:

Pull A Public Container Image

Here’s a simple kubernetes cluster.

It has access to pull images from the public internet.

item.title: Publishing Docs as Code
item.description:
Concluding a delve into docs as code…

If you wanna publish chosen articles to Medium without the tedium, Part 3, this how-to guide can help you.

item.title: How To Post To Medium Without The Tedium
item.description:
You’ve got a Markdown build pipeline and you wanna publish to Medium without the tedium!

If you wanna publish a chosen article to Medium, not an entire static site, this how to is for you.

item.title: Analysing A Docs As Code Pipeline
item.description:
The Hemingway Editor says that sentence is:

  1. hard to read (yellow highlight), and;
  2. uses passive voice (green highlight).

As an noob writer, I frequently make those mistakes, so that kind of feedback really helps me.

item.title: Docs-as-code Build Pipeline Explained: From Crazy to Consistent
item.description: Here’s an example docs-as-code build pipeline. It describes a process for checking and testing documentation written in markdown format. After committing and pushing markdown changes to Github, the pipeline will run. The output of the build pipeline is a complete static HTML site.

item.title: How To Create a Docs as Code Build Pipeline
item.description:
Before you publish written content, you wanna check spelling, lint markdown, improve prose, and fix broken links.

This approach uses a builder image specialised for your docs-as-code workflow. Pre-install and configure your docs-as-code tools in the image. Finally, use them in synergy in a build and test pipeline.

item.title: Creating A Docs As Code Pipeline
item.description:
Have you thought about managing docs as code?

Before you publish written content, you wanna check spelling, lint markdown, improve prose, and fix broken links. The idea of docs-as-code is to version control, build, test, and deploy your documentation using pull requests, continuous integration and continuous deployment. The value is you can make reading and writing easier and more fun though standardising language, formatting and style.

item.title: World Cup Troubleshootout
item.description: Every four years the Fifa World Cup ignites my passion to troubleshoot a bunch of technical problems so that I can enjoy the tournament in the same way I grew up - with highly-biased English commentary.

item.title: How to Enable HDMI on Thinkpad X1 Extreme Gen 4
item.description: You’re running Ubuntu on a Thinkpad X1 Extreme (Gen 4). You wanna use HDMI, but the TV or monitor says something like “Check Device Power”.

item.title: An Explainer Using the 1000 Most Used English Words
item.description: The other day, I learned about the Up-Goer Five Editor - a text editor that challenges you to explain something using only the 1000 most-used English words.

item.title: What is Attribute Based Encryption? ABE explained using the 1000 most used English words
item.description: Attribute-Based Encryption (ABE) is a way to lock something away using “key”-words. Only people who have the keywords in their key can open the lock. The name of the approach comes from; ‘Attributes’, which are really keywords like those you enter into the search box; and, ‘Encryption’, which hides the thing. It’s different from other ways to hide something, because when you pick the keywords, that’s also ‘Access Control’. That’s great, because with ABE, you can store all the things at some outsider place without the need for trusted guards!
